posts.name: Dale Williams
posts.cooked:
Steamy/swampy evening in NYC, but a good crew of old-school winegeeks gathered to welcome our old friend Arv’s visit. Dinner at La Mangeoire, under new ownership but with few changes, at least to the charcuturie and chicken I had.
2013 Jamet Cotes du Rhone Blanc
I’m not much of a white Rhone guy, but this was quite good. Sweet citrus and musky fruits, bright and fresh, good balance, a little almond note like an Italian white. B/B+
2011 Guigal ‘La Doriane’ Condrieu
Floral, oaky, moderate acids. Others liked more than I did. B-

The blind red.
Came across a bit hot to start, framboise and herbs, big ripe wine. I initially thought CA, then started thinking ‘03 or ‘07 CdP, but Paul guessed ‘03 so I ended up guessing Aussie GSM. But Nano stayed on theme, I was surprised this was a supposed cooler vintage wine. Others liked more than I did.
2004 P. Usseglio “Mon Aieul” Chateauneuf-du-Pape B-

First flight
1989 Beaucastel Chateauneuf-du-Pape
Off bottle. Some thought corked, which I didn’t get at all, more like a secondary fermentation. High/volatile acids, screechy, with a hint of fizziness/spritziness. C-
1989 Pegau Reserve Chateauneuf-du-Pape
Lovely showing, nice texture, plummy red fruits with leather, herbs. and forest floor. I’m used to brett in my limited experience with Pegau but other than maybe the leather nothing bretty to me. A-/B+

1990 Clos des Papes Chateauneuf-du-Pape
Red fruits, garrigue, a little mocha note. No hurry on these. Still going strong at end of evening. B+/A-

Second flight
1995 Beaucastel Chateauneuf-du-Papel
Mild barnyard funk, black plum fruit, mature and ready. B

2000 Mordoree “ Reine des Bois” Chateauneuf-du-Pape
I thought ‘00 was early drinking vintage, but this needs time. Raspberries, mocha, some anise notes. Tannic. A bit riper than I’d prefer. B

Third flight
2001 Clos des Papes Chateauneuf-du-Pape
Big young, good acids, very firm tannins. I liked this a lot though thought it could use some time. B+/A-
2001 Pegau Chateauneuf-du-Pape
Darker fruits, lots of herb, but with some fresh barnyard. I don;t mind a little cowpatty, but really prefer it a bit dried out than perfectly fresh, B


Northern- 4th Flight
1999 Clape Cornas
Fresh, smoke over black fruits, lots of complexity and nice length. A-/B+

1999 Texier Hermitage
Black fruits, peppery spice, some leather and cocoa. Good acids but not screechy, nice length. I liked more than some others. A-/B+

Some cheese to finish.

We did our usual 3/2/1 voting, the Pegau came out on top
1989 Pegau Reserve Chateauneuf-du-Pape 11
1990 Clos des Papes Chateauneuf-du-Pape 9
1999 Clape Cornas 6
1999 Texier Hermitage 6
2004 P. Usseglio “Mon Aieul” Chateauneuf-du-Pape 3
2001 Clos des Papes Chateauneuf-du-Pape 3
2000 Mordoree “ Reine des Bois” Chateauneuf-du-Pape 2
2001 Pegau Chateauneuf-du-Pape 0
1989 Beaucastel Chateauneuf-du-Pape 0
1995 Beaucastel Chateauneuf-du-Pape 0
(looks like 2 votes went astray)

Good night, and great to see Arv.

Grade disclaimer: I’m a very easy grader, basically A is an excellent wine, B a good wine, C drinkable. Anything below C means I wouldn’t drink at a party where it was only choice.Furthermore, I offer no promises of objectivity, accuracy, and certainly not of consistency.

edited to correct 2 typos

posts.name: Markus S
posts.cooked:
Dale Williams:

****1989 Pegau Reserve Chateauneuf-du-Pape
…I’m used to brett in my limited experience with Pegau but other than maybe the leather nothing bretty to me. A-/B+

I think the 89 came from before the time they became infected. None in the 1990 or 91 either.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Markus S:

I think the 89 came from before the time they became infected. None in the 1990 or 91 either.

While I absolutely love both the '89 and '90 Pégaüs, I think ‘none’ might be over-stating the case. We had the 1990 last week. The wine is beautifully aromatically integrated, but there is surely some brett in the mix: leather, grilled meat etc.

posts.name: Marc Hanes
posts.cooked: Fun time, thanks to all. I really liked both the 89 Pegau and 90 Clos des Papes and the Clape was in a very good place. Had never had a white Jamet before so that was a treat. Good to see everyone.

posts.name: Paul Jaouen
posts.cooked: I was really disappointed in the bad bottle of 89 Beau. I was so looking forward to that wine side by side with the Pegau. Fun evening!

posts.name: Ramon Cabrera
posts.cooked:
I also was surprised with the blind 2004 Ussseglio ‘Mon Aueil’ about being big, oaky and with prominent alcohol, being from a cooler vintage. But then I also recalled having had a few from the late 90’s vintages that were big and oaky. Must be the house style?

Always great to see and catch up with good, old NYC-wine enthusiasts stalwart Arv.

I’ve always loved the ’89 Pegau, but I thought last night the ’90 Clos des Papes just eclipsed it as my WOTN. The relative lighter-bodied and slightly more delicate Clos des Papes was more supplementary of my good grilled lamb chops.

The 1999 Texier Hermitage showed just too much of its stemmy and overly rustic side last night. It was just too harsh, although I liked the savory characteristics. But the 1999 Clape Cornas, I thought, showed a more even-handed restraint in the rustic scheme of things. The Clape was the more elegant Northern Rhone while not losing its Cornas traits with enough mineral, earthy, savory and cool syrah notes. This was also great with my medium-rare lamb chops.

It was really nice to meet up with the gang, especially with all the fun stories that everybody shared.

posts.name: Arv R@()
posts.cooked:
Really good time, quite the spectrum of wines, and thanks for arranging.

I wasn’t really feeling well (still am not) and nothing really smelled/tasted right to me.

Maybe when the humidity or air pressure are like that things don’t show well.

My notes are in a suitcase so I’ll post them later.

Sadly, I’m still trying to get home, having left for the airport about 30 hours ago, and floundering in a lounge somewhere, trying to ignore the bad wine selection.

posts.name: Jonathan Loesberg
posts.cooked: The Usseglio Mon Aieul is aged in cement tank. I don’t know what caused the impression of oakiness, which I don’t doubt was there, but it wasn’t oak.

posts.name: Dale Williams
posts.cooked: I didn’t think the Mon Aieul was oaky, but it seemed much riper than I would have expected from '04. I think it said 14.5%, not so high for CdP, but it tasted hotter to me.

posts.name: Ramon Cabrera
posts.cooked: It was probably my note that triggered the oak reaction. Well, I might have overdone it with the oak comment (admitting that my TN is from memory), but some published notes state that while cement was the dominant vessel, there were some aging done in oak foudres.

posts.name: Jonathan Loesberg
posts.cooked: Very few CdP domaines use only cement or inox (Charvin is one). And Usseglio does use barriques on the Deux Freres as well as foudres on their normal cuvee. But the Danish website confirms what I remember their telling me on my visits–the Aieul is all in cement. For what it’s worth, I am pretty oak averse with regard to barriques, but, while foudres do provide more oxygenation and wood tannins, they don’t mark the taste of the wine for me.

posts.name: Paul Jaouen
posts.cooked:
R Cabrera:

It was probably my note that triggered the oak reaction. Well, I might have overdone it with the oak comment (admitting that my TN is from memory), but some published notes state that while cement was the dominant vessel, there were some aging done in oak foudres.

You probably had oak residue on your palate from the Doriane.

posts.name: Matt Richman
posts.cooked: Kickin’ it old skool with Arv in NYC!

posts.name: Marc Hanes
posts.cooked:
Matt Richman:

Kickin’ it old skool with Arv in NYC!

Hey Matt! Hope all is well, give Kristin a hug for me for old time’s sake!

If you ever hit NYC hopefully we can do an offline too. Hayes and Matthew made it to my wedding celebration party, it was great to see them…

posts.name: Arv R@()
posts.cooked:
Great time, and thanks for so many awesome wines.

Sadly, I wasn’t feeling great, and in particular my sense of smell has been impaired for a week, which also affects my taste as well. So nothing really showed (to me) as well as I would have expected. On the bright side some of the wines I’d never had were revelatory even with my plugged nose.

13 Jamet blanc [Cotes du Rhone] I didn’t know they made a white but it was good. Straw like notes, not very dense, and despite being a Northern CdR no viognier in here. Tank aged I believe?

11 Guigal ‘La Doriane’ [Condrieu] Vanilla, woody, full bodied, low acid, some notes of anaesthetic, glycerin on the palate. long 40 second finish. It really was just too hot for this despite being in an ice bucket for a half hour.

04 Pierre Usseglio ‘Mon Aieul’ [CNDP] Served blind and the table was generally confounded by it. We tried guessing whether it was young, or old – cool climate or hot and so on. I thought it was a California Rhone Ranger, maybe something like an Edmunds St John or something. Others guessed along those lines too, but there was some guesses for Oz GSM or CNDP. I picked up some blackberry coulis, some stewed fruits. It was big framed, and kind of flabby with a nutty hazelnut tone. I was surprised to see that it was a cool year CNDP tete du cuvee. We had a discussion about the whole concept, and I reiterated my long held view: I’d rather producers just make the best regular blend they can and eschew the max points that come from a small 300 case luxe offering. This was decanted by the way.

89 Pegau [CNDP] 13.7% abv I think. classic barnyard, funky, sneakers but then has sweet fruit and good depth. I liked it

89 Beaucastel [CNDP] some tasters felt this was corked. I thought it was funky, but still picked up fruit and considered it ok. I’m not very TCA sensitive, and wines like Pegau/Beaucastel which are not at a UC Davis level of cleanliness are particularly challenging I think in that regard.

90 Clos des Papes [CNDP] I thought this was sweet, silky, medium bodied. It did have some figgy/pruney/raisiny aromas which sounds worse when I transcribe them from my hotel notepads, but at the time I liked it. It was my top 3 wines of the night.

95 Beaucastel [CNDP] my notes are short on this - tannic and clenched up. Generally I don’t think CNDP’s need much decanting but perhaps this would have benefited from that?

00 Mordoree ‘Reine de Boisse’ [CNDP] Anther tete du cuvee offering makes an appearance. I’d never had this before either. Keeps on richening up as it opens up. Long length of finish. My notes are losing detail, as the impact of alcohol takes a toll, but I noted that this was also one of my 3 favorite wines, although just about everything was really good.

01 Pegau [CNDP] Rich nutty, Dark and cloudy. I’ve had this a lot and it was in line with prior experiences.

01 Clos de Papes [CNDP] Excellent and lots of grip still. No hurry on this one.

and now for a few flagships from the Northern end of the Valley

99 Clape [Cornas] I was very excited to try this as I’d not had one before. Rich, lots of verve, tannic, not filtered so seemed kind of cloudy. I had some rubber notes. My notes don’t really do it justice, but a very impressive wine. Tough to choose from so many but again one of my top 3 choices for the evening.

99 Texier [Hermitage] Probably the darkest wine of the night. Smoky and Barbeque flavors here. Unlike most other Texier wines I’ve had this was not shrill and screechy. Maybe time smooths out that low residual sugar aspect of their wines so that with age they taste more balanced. But I also get that people have different palates, and for some, the brightness in youth is desirable. Or it could also be that a fine year like 1999 gave them enough ripeness to make it all work out. I don’t have any Texier’s but might keep an eye out for bottlings from hot years.

It was great catching up with everyone and meeting the effusive Leo, as well as trying so many standout Rhones. I just wish I’d been feeling better so I could have enjoyed these more. Summertime in NYC is a tough time for these big boys.
